fragments.content: Borderline Personality Disorder (BPD) is characterized by pervasive instability in interpersonal relationships, self-image, affects, and marked impulsivity. Core features: frantic efforts to avoid abandonment, unstable/intense relationships (idealization/devaluation), identity disturbance, impulsivity in ≥2 self-damaging areas (spending, sex, substance use, reckless driving, binge eating), recurrent suicidal behavior/self-mutilation, affective instability, chronic emptiness, inappropriate intense anger, transient stress-related paranoia/dissociation. Prevalence ~1-3%, more commonly diagnosed in women. Pathophysiology involves emotional dysregulation, amygdala hyperreactivity, prefrontal cortex dysfunction, genetic factors (~40-60% heritability), and environmental factors (childhood trauma common).

Psychotherapy is first-line: Dialectical Behavior Therapy (DBT) has strongest evidence, targeting emotion regulation, distress tolerance, interpersonal effectiveness, mindfulness through individual therapy, skills groups, phone coaching, therapist consultation team. Other effective therapies: Mentalization-Based Treatment (MBT), Transference-Focused Psychotherapy (TFP), Schema-Focused Therapy. No medications approved specifically for BPD; pharmacotherapy targets specific symptoms (mood stabilizers for affective instability, atypical antipsychotics for cognitive-perceptual symptoms, SSRIs for comorbid depression/anxiety). Avoid polypharmacy. Hospitalization reserved for acute safety crises. Long-term prognosis generally favorable with appropriate treatment.

DSM-5-TR BPD criteria: Pervasive pattern of instability in relationships, self-image, affects, impulsivity beginning by early adulthood, present in various contexts, indicated by ≥5 of: frantic efforts to avoid abandonment, unstable/intense relationships (idealization/devaluation), identity disturbance, impulsivity in ≥2 potentially self-damaging areas, recurrent suicidal behavior/gestures/threats/self-mutilation, affective instability due to mood reactivity, chronic emptiness, inappropriate intense anger/difficulty controlling anger, transient stress-related paranoid ideation/severe dissociative symptoms. Not better explained by another mental disorder, substance effects, or medical condition.
